summary:
The fall of Khartoum on 26 January 1885, when Mahdist forces stormed the city and killed British General Charles Gordon, was one of the most dramatic and politically significant events of the Victorian imperial era. Gordon's death, coming just days before a British relief expedition reached the city, created a national outcry in Britain and made Gordon a martyred hero of empire.

General Gordon had been sent to Khartoum in January 1884 by the British government to oversee the withdrawal of Egyptian garrisons from Sudan in the face of the [[event:2596|Mahdist uprising]]. Gordon, a Christian soldier of deeply held religious convictions and considerable personal charisma, defied his instructions and attempted to hold Khartoum, hoping to provoke a British commitment to Sudan's defense. The Mahdist forces besieged the city and Gordon held out for 317 days, communicating with Cairo and London through a series of messages while the besieging forces tightened their grip.

Prime Minister Gladstone's government was deeply reluctant to commit to a military campaign in Sudan, and the relief expedition sent up the Nile moved far too slowly. The advance boats of the relief force arrived at Khartoum on 28 January to find the city in Mahdist hands and Gordon dead. The city had fallen two days before their arrival. Whether the relief force could have saved Gordon had it moved faster — and whether Gladstone's government was culpably negligent — became the subject of fierce political controversy. Gordon was commemorated throughout the Empire in statues, paintings, and commemorations, and 'Gordon of Khartoum' became one of the defining imperial heroes of the Victorian age.
